metadata.title: Rotate an electron all the way around and it comes back wrong
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=spinor/half-angle phase; CRIMSON=sign-flipped/wrong state; GOLD=dial marker
metadata.note: Half-angle rotation. Spinor picks up -1 after 360 degrees, needs 720 to restore.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Rotate an electron a full circle. Bring it back to where it started. It is now the negative of what it was. You have to go around twice to restore it.

card.headline:
Rotate an electron all
the way around and it
comes back wrong

beats.narration_text: Classical objects come back after one full turn. A top, a ball, a planet — rotate 360 degrees and you are back exactly where you started. So why does a quantum spin-half particle need two full turns to return to itself?

card.headline:
Why does an electron need
two full turns — not one —
to come back to itself?

beats.narration_text: A spin-half state is a two-component complex vector. When you rotate it by angle phi about the z-axis, the rotation operator multiplies the top component by e to the minus i phi over two, and the bottom by e to the plus i phi over two. The angle that multiplies the spinor is half the rotation angle.

Watch the phase dial. As the physical rotation climbs from zero to 360 degrees, the half-angle argument climbs from zero to only 180 degrees. When the physical arrow returns to the top, the phase dial is only halfway around.

At phi equals 360 degrees the phase argument hits pi. And e to the i pi equals negative one. So the spinor that returns to its start position has been multiplied by negative one — it is the negative of what it was before the rotation. One full turn gives minus the original state.

Continue rotating. At 720 degrees the physical arrow has gone around twice. The phase argument has now climbed to 2 pi. And e to the i times 2 pi equals positive one. The spinor is restored. Two full turns return the spinor to exactly its starting value.

Is this observable? The sign flip itself does not change any probability — you square the wavefunction to get probabilities, so minus psi gives the same result as psi. But the sign matters when you interfere two spinors. Mix the original with its 360-rotated negative and they cancel.

This is not a quirk of the math. Neutron interferometry experiments have measured the minus sign directly: split a neutron beam, rotate one branch by 360 degrees, recombine, and the intensity drops by destructive interference. The two-turn rule is real.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is not a quirk of the math. Neutron interferometry experiments have measured the minus sign directly: split a neutron beam, rotate one branch by 360 degree

beats.narration_text: The half-angle structure comes from SU(2), the group of 2-by-2 unitary matrices. SU(2) is the double cover of SO(3), the rotation group of ordinary space. Every ordinary rotation corresponds to two spinor rotations, one for the state and one for the negative state. Classical objects live in SO(3). Quantum spin lives in SU(2).
The rotation operator for spin-half carries a half-angle. A 360-degree turn gives a phase of pi — a sign flip. A 720-degree turn gives a phase of 2 pi — the identity. Two turns to return.

card.headline:
Half-angle in the exponent.
360° = sign flip.
720° = restored.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Math for Quantum Physics series.
